subject: Math
description: Classify objects into given categories; count the numbers of objects in each category and sort the categories by count (Limit category counts to be less than or equal to 10).
description_plain: Your child can sort objects into groups, count how many are in each group (up to 10), and put the groups in order by size.

subject: Math
description: Directly compare two objects, with a measureable attribute in common, to see which object has "more of"/"less of" the attribute, and describe the difference.
description_plain: Your child can compare two objects to see which one has more or less of something and explain the difference.

subject: Math
description: Describe measurable attributes of objects, such as length or weight. Describe several measurable attributes of a single object.
description_plain: Your child can describe things about objects that can be measured, like how long or heavy they are, and talk about more than one of these things for the same object.

subject: Math
description: Compose and decompose numbers from 11 to 19 into ten ones and some further ones, (e.g. by using objects or drawings, and record each composition or decomposition by a drawing or equation (e.g. 10 + 8 = 18 and 19 = 10 + 9); ); understand that these numbers are composed of ten ones and one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine ones.
description_plain: Your child can break apart numbers from 11 to 19 into ten ones and some more ones, using objects or drawings to show it.

subject: Math
description: Fluently (efficiently, accurately, and flexibly) add and subtract within 5.
description_plain: Your child can quickly and correctly add and subtract numbers up to 5.

subject: Math
description: For any number from 1 to 9, find the number that makes 10 when added to the given number, (e.g. by using objects or drawings, and record the answer with a drawing or equation.).
description_plain: Your child can find the number that adds to another number from 1 to 9 to make 10, using objects or drawings.

subject: Math
description: Decompose numbers less than or equal to 10 into pairs in more than one way, (e.g. by using objects or drawings, and record each decomposition by a drawing or equation (e.g. 5 = 2 + 3 and 5 = 4 + 1 )).
description_plain: Your child can break numbers up to 10 into pairs in different ways, using objects or drawings.

subject: Math
description: Solve addition and subtraction word problems, and add and subtract within 10, (e.g. by using objects or drawings to represent the problem.)
description_plain: Your child can solve simple addition and subtraction story problems using objects or drawings.

subject: Math
description: Represent addition and subtraction with objects, fingers, mental images, drawings, sounds (e.g. claps), acting out situations, verbal explanations, expressions, or equations.
description_plain: Your child can show addition and subtraction using objects, fingers, pictures, sounds, acting, or words.

subject: Math
description: Compare two numbers between 1 and 10 presented as written numerals.
description_plain: Your child can compare two numbers from 1 to 10 when they are written as numerals.

subject: Math
description: Identify whether the number of objects in one group is greater than, less than, or equal to the number of objects in another group, (e.g. by using matching and counting strategies.) Include groups with up to ten objects.
description_plain: Your child can tell if one group of up to 10 objects has more, less, or the same number as another group.

subject: Math
description: Count to answer "how many?" up to20 concrete or pictorial objects arranged in a line, a rectangular array, or a circle, or as many as 10 objects in a scattered configuration (subitizing); given a number from 1 to 20, count out that many objects.
description_plain: Your child can count up to 20 objects arranged in different ways and can count out a given number of objects up to 20.

subject: Math
description: Represent a number of objects with a written numeral 0-20 (with 0 representing a count of no objects).
description_plain: Your child can write numbers from 0 to 20 to show how many objects there are, including zero for no objects.

subject: Math
description: Understand that each successive number name refers to a quantity that is one larger.
description_plain: Your child understands that when you say numbers in order, each number is one more than the one before.

subject: Math
description: Understand that the last number name said tells the number of objects counted. The number of objects is the same regardless of their arrangement or the order in which they were counted.
description_plain: Your child understands that the last number said when counting tells how many objects there are, no matter how they are arranged or counted.

subject: Math
description: When counting objects, say each number's name in sequential order, pairing each object with one and only one number name and each number name with one and only one object.
description_plain: Your child can count objects by saying each number once and matching each object to one number.

subject: Math
description: Understand the relationship between numbers and quantities; connect counting to cardinality.
description_plain: Your child understands how numbers relate to amounts and connects counting to knowing how many things there are.

subject: Math
description: Read and write numerals from 0 to 20.
description_plain: Your child can read and write numbers from 0 to 20.

subject: Math
description: Count forward beginning from a given number within the known sequence (instead of having to begin at 1).
description_plain: Your child can count forward starting from any number they know, not just from 1.

subject: Math
description: Count to 100 by ones and by tens and identify as a growth pattern.
description_plain: Your child can count to 100 by ones and tens and notice the pattern in counting.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the best tools to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics
description_plain: Model with mathematics

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Partition circles and rectangles into two and four equal shares, describe the shares using the words halves, fourths, and quarters, and use the phrases half of, fourth of, and quarter of.
description_plain: Your child can divide circles and rectangles into two or four equal parts, use words like halves and quarters to describe them, and understand that more parts mean smaller pieces.

subject: Math
description: Compose two-dimensional shapes (rectangles, squares, trapezoids, triangles, half-circles, and quarter-circles) or three-dimensional shapes (cubes, right rectangular prisms, right circular cones, and right circular cylinders) to create a composite shape, and compose new shapes from the composite shape. Students do not need to learn formal names such as "right rectangular prism."
description_plain: Your child can put together flat shapes like squares and triangles or solid shapes like cubes and cones to make new shapes, and then make new shapes from those.

subject: Math
description: Distinguish between defining attributes (e.g. triangles are closed and three-sided) versus non-defining attributes (e.g. color, orientation, overall size); build and draw shapes that possess defining attributes.
description_plain: Your child can tell what makes a shape a certain kind (like a triangle has three sides) and can build and draw shapes with those important features.

subject: Math
description: Organize, represent, and interpret data with up to three categories; ask and answer questions about the total number of data points, how many in each category, and how many more or less are in one category than in another.
description_plain: Your child can collect and show information in up to three groups, and ask and answer questions about how many items are in each group and how they compare.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time in hours and half-hours using analog and digital clocks.
description_plain: Your child can tell and write time to the hour and half-hour using both clocks with hands and digital clocks.

subject: Math
description: Express the length of an object as a whole number of length units, by laying multiple copies of a shorter object (the length unit) end to end; understand that the length measurement of an object is the number of same-size length units that span it with no gaps or overlaps. Limit to contexts where the object being measured is spanned by a whole number of length units with no gaps or overlaps.
description_plain: Your child can measure how long something is by lining up smaller objects of the same size without gaps or overlaps and count how many it takes.

subject: Math
description: Order three objects by length; compare the lengths of two objects indirectly by using a third object.
description_plain: Your child can put three objects in order from shortest to longest and compare two objects by using a third object as a reference.

subject: Math
description: Subtract multiples of 10 in the range 10 to 90 from multiples of 10 in the range 10 to 90 (positive or zero differences), using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; relate the strategy to a written method and explain the reasoning used.
description_plain: Your child can subtract multiples of 10 from 10 to 90 using drawings or objects and explain how they solved it.

subject: Math
description: Given a two-digit number, mentally find 10 more or 10 less than the number, without having to count; explain the reasoning used.
description_plain: Your child can quickly find 10 more or 10 less than any two-digit number and explain how they know.

subject: Math
description: Understanding that when adding two-digit numbers, combine like base-ten units such as tens and tens, ones and ones; and sometimes it is necessary to compose a ten.
description_plain: Your child understands that when adding two-digit numbers, they add tens to tens and ones to ones, and sometimes make a new ten.

subject: Math
description: Adding a two-digit number and a multiple of 10
description_plain: Your child can add a two-digit number and a multiple of 10.

subject: Math
description: Adding a two-digit number and a one-digit number
description_plain: Your child can add a two-digit number and a one-digit number.

subject: Math
description: Add within 100 using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; relate the strategy to a written method and explain the reasoning used including:
description_plain: Your child can add numbers up to 100 using objects or drawings and explain how they solved the problem.

subject: Math
description: Compare two two-digit numbers based on meanings of the tens and ones digits, recording the results of comparisons with the relational symbols >, <, =, and ≠.
description_plain: Your child can compare two two-digit numbers by looking at their tens and ones and use >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.

subject: Math
description: Show flexibility in composing and decomposing tens and ones (e.g. 20 can be composed from 2 tens or 1 ten and 10 ones, or 20 ones.)
description_plain: Your child can break apart and put together tens and ones in different ways, like seeing 20 as 2 tens or 1 ten and 10 ones.

subject: Math
description: The numbers 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 refer to one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine tens (and 0 ones).
description_plain: Your child knows that numbers like 10, 20, 30, and so on mean one, two, three, etc., groups of ten.

subject: Math
description: The numbers from 11 to 19 are composed of a ten and one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine ones.
description_plain: Your child understands that numbers from 11 to 19 are made of one ten and some ones.

subject: Math
description: 10 can be thought of as a grouping of ten ones—called a "ten."
description_plain: Your child understands that 10 is a group of ten ones called a 'ten.'

subject: Math
description: Understand that the two digits of a two-digit number represent amounts of tens and ones. Understand the following as special cases:
description_plain: Your child understands that two-digit numbers have tens and ones, and knows special cases about these numbers.

subject: Math
description: Count to 120 (recognizing growth and repeating patterns), starting at any number less than 120. In this range, read and write numerals and represent a number of objects with a written numeral.
description_plain: Your child can count to 120 starting from any number below 120, read and write numbers, and match numbers to objects.

subject: Math
description: Using related equations, Determine the unknown whole number in an addition or subtraction equation.
description_plain: Your child can find the missing number in addition or subtraction problems using related facts.

subject: Math
description: Understand the meaning of the equal sign (the value is the same on both sides of the equal sign), and determine if equations involving addition and subtraction are true or false.
description_plain: Your child understands that the equal sign means both sides are the same and can tell if addition or subtraction equations are true or false.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract within 20, demonstrating fluency (efficiently, accurately, and flexibly) for addition and subtraction within 10. Use mental strategies such as counting on; making ten (e.g. 8+6 = 8+2+4 = 10+4 = 14); decomposing a number leading to a ten (e.g. 13-4 = 13-3-1 = 10-1 = 9); using the relationship between addition and subtraction (e.g. knowing that 8+4 = 12, one knows 12-8 = 4); and creating equivalent but easier or known sums (e.g. adding 6+7 by creating the known equivalent 6+6+1 = 12+1 = 13).
description_plain: Your child can quickly and correctly add and subtract numbers up to 20 using mental tricks like counting on or making ten.

subject: Math
description: Relate counting to addition and subtraction (e.g. by counting on 2 to add 2, counting back 1 to subtract 1).
description_plain: Your child can use counting to help add or subtract numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand subtraction as an unknown-addend problem. For example, subtract 10-8 by finding the number that makes 10 when added to 8.
description_plain: Your child understands subtraction by thinking about what number added to the smaller number makes the bigger number.

subject: Math
description: Apply (not necessary to name) properties of operations as strategies to add and subtract.
description_plain: Your child uses simple math rules to help add and subtract without needing to name the rules.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems that call for addition of three whole numbers whose sum is less than or equal to 20, (e.g. by using objects, drawings, and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.)
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that add three whole numbers with a total of 20 or less.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction within 20 to solve word problems involving situations of adding to, taking from, putting together, taking apart, and comparing, with unknowns in all positions, (e.g. by using objects, drawings, and situation equations and/or solution equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.)
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems with addition and subtraction up to 20, including problems where the missing number can be anywhere.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the best tools to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics
description_plain: Model with mathematics

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Partition circles and rectangles into two, three, or four equal shares, describe the shares using the words halves, thirds, half of, a third of, etc., and describe the whole as two halves, three thirds, four fourths. Note: fraction notation 1/2, 1/3, 1/4 is not expected at this grade level. Recognize that equal shares of identical wholes need not have the same shape.
description_plain: Your child can divide circles and rectangles into two, three, or four equal parts, use words like halves and thirds to describe them, and understand that equal parts can look different.

subject: Math
description: Partition a rectangle into rows and columns of same-size squares and count to find the total number of them.
description_plain: Your child can divide a rectangle into rows and columns of equal squares and count how many squares there are.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and draw shapes having specified attributes, such as a given number of angles or a given number of equal faces. Identify triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons, and cubes.
description_plain: Your child can recognize and draw shapes by their features, like how many angles or equal sides they have, and can name shapes like triangles and cubes.

subject: Math
description: Identify coins and bills and their values.
description_plain: Your child can recognize coins and bills and know how much money each is worth.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving dollar bills, quarters, dimes, nickels, and pennies, using $ and ¢ symbols appropriately (Do not use decimal point, if showing 25 cents, use the word cents or ¢).
description_plain: Your child can solve money problems using dollar bills and coins, and use $ and ¢ symbols correctly.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time from analog and digital clocks to the nearest five minutes.
description_plain: Your child can tell and write time to the nearest five minutes using both analog and digital clocks.

subject: Math
description: Represent whole numbers as lengths from 0 on a number line diagram with equally spaced points corresponding to the numbers 0, 1, 2, …, and represent whole-number sums and differences within 100 on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can show whole numbers on a number line starting at zero and use the number line to add and subtract numbers up to 100.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction within 100 to solve one- and two-step word problems involving lengths that are given in the same units, e.g. by using drawings (such as drawings of rulers) and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems about length using addition and subtraction within 100, using drawings or equations.

subject: Math
description: Measure to determine how much longer one object is than another, expressing the length difference in terms of a standard length unit (inches, feet, centimeters, and meters).
description_plain: Your child can measure how much longer one object is than another using standard units like inches or centimeters.

subject: Math
description: Estimate lengths using whole units of inches, feet, centimeters, and meters.
description_plain: Your child can estimate lengths using whole inches, feet, centimeters, or meters.

subject: Math
description: Measure the length of an object twice, using length units of different lengths for the two measurements; describe how the two measurements relate to the size of the unit chosen.
description_plain: Your child can measure the same object twice using different units and explain how the measurements relate to the size of the units.

subject: Math
description: Measure the length of an object by selecting and using appropriate tools such as rulers, yardsticks, meter sticks, and measuring tapes.
description_plain: Your child can measure the length of an object using tools like rulers, yardsticks, or measuring tapes.

subject: Math
description: Draw a picture graph and a bar graph (with single-unit scale) to represent a data set with up to four categories. Solve simple put-together, take-apart, and compare problems using information presented in a bar graph
description_plain: Your child can draw picture and bar graphs with up to four groups and use them to solve simple problems.

subject: Math
description: Generate measurement data by measuring lengths of several objects to the nearest whole unit, or by making repeated measurements of the same object using different units. Show the measurements by making a line plot, where the horizontal scale is marked off in whole-number units.
description_plain: Your child can measure objects to the nearest whole unit and show the results on a line plot.

subject: Math
description: Explain why addition and subtraction strategies work using place value and the properties of operations. The explanations given may be supported by drawings or objects.
description_plain: Your child can explain why their addition and subtraction methods work using place value and math properties, sometimes with drawings or objects.

subject: Math
description: Mentally add 10 or 100 to a given number 100 – 900, and mentally subtract 10 or 100 from a given number 100 – 900.
description_plain: Your child can add or subtract 10 or 100 in their head for numbers between 100 and 900.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract within 1000, using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; relate the strategy to a written method. Understand that in adding or subtracting three-digit numbers, like base-ten units such as hundreds and hundreds, tens and tens, ones and ones are used; and sometimes it is necessary to compose or decompose tens or hundreds.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 1000 using drawings or objects and understands how to add or subtract hundreds, tens, and ones.

subject: Math
description: Add up to four two-digit numbers using strategies based on place value and properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can add up to four two-digit numbers using place value and math rules.

subject: Math
description: Fluently (efficiently, accurately, and flexibly) add and subtract within 100 using strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction (e.g. composing/decomposing by like base-10 units, using friendly or benchmark numbers, using related equations, compensation, number line, etc.).
description_plain: Your child can quickly and accurately add and subtract numbers within 100 using place value and other strategies.

subject: Math
description: Compare two three-digit numbers based on meanings of the hundreds, tens, and ones digits, using >, <, =, and ≠ relational symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can compare two three-digit numbers using >, <, =, or ≠ by looking at hundreds, tens, and ones.

subject: Math
description: Read and write numbers within 1000 using base-ten numerals, number names, expanded form, and unit form.
description_plain: Your child can read and write numbers up to 999 in different ways, like standard form, words, and expanded form.

subject: Math
description: Count within 1000; skip-count by 2s, 5s, 10s, and 100s; explain and generalize the patterns.
description_plain: Your child can count to 1000, skip count by 2s, 5s, 10s, and 100s, and explain the patterns they see.

subject: Math
description: Show flexibility in composing and decomposing hundreds, tens and ones (e.g. 207 can be composed from 2 hundreds 7 ones OR 20 tens 7 ones OR 207 ones OR 1 hundred 10 tens 7 ones OR 1 hundred 9 tens 17 ones, etc.)
description_plain: Your child can break apart and put together numbers in hundreds, tens, and ones in many different ways.

subject: Math
description: The numbers 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900 refer to one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine hundreds.
description_plain: Your child understands that numbers like 100, 200, 300, up to 900 mean one to nine hundreds with zero tens and ones.

subject: Math
description: 100 can be thought of as a bundle of ten tens—called a "hundred."
description_plain: Your child understands that 100 is a group of ten tens called a 'hundred.'

subject: Math
description: Understand that the three digits of a three-digit number represent amounts of hundreds, tens, and ones; (e.g. 706 equals 7 hundreds, 0 tens, and 6 ones.) Understand the following as special cases:
description_plain: Your child understands that three-digit numbers have hundreds, tens, and ones, like 706 means 7 hundreds, 0 tens, and 6 ones.

subject: Math
description: Use addition to find the total number of objects arranged in rectangular arrays with up to 5 rows and up to 5 columns; write an equation to express the total as a sum of equal addends.
description_plain: Your child can use addition to find the total number of objects arranged in rows and columns (up to 5 rows and 5 columns) and write an equation showing equal groups added together.

subject: Math
description: Determine whether a group of objects (up to 20) has an odd or even number of members, (e.g. by pairing objects or counting them by 2s); write an equation to express an even number as a sum of two equal addends.
description_plain: Your child can tell if a group of up to 20 objects has an odd or even number by pairing or counting by twos and write an equation showing even numbers as two equal parts.

subject: Math
description: Fluently (efficiently, accurately, and flexibly) add and subtract within 20 using mental strategies (counting on, making a ten, decomposing a number, creating an equivalent but easier and known sum, and using the relationship between addition and subtraction).
description_plain: Your child can quickly and accurately add and subtract numbers up to 20 using mental strategies like making ten or breaking numbers apart.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction within 100 to solve one- and two-step word problems involving situations of adding to, taking from, putting together, taking apart, and comparing, with unknowns in all positions, (e.g. by using drawings and situation equations and/or solution equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.)
description_plain: Your child can solve one- and two-step word problems with addition and subtraction within 100 by using drawings or simple equations with a missing number.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the best tools to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics
description_plain: Model with mathematics

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Compare two decimals to hundredths by reasoning about their size. Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two decimals refer to the same whole. Record the results of comparisons with the relational symbols >, < =, or ≠, and justify the conclusions, (e.g. by using a visual model.).
description_plain: Your child can compare two decimals to hundredths by thinking about their size, write >, =, or < to show the comparison, and explain why, using pictures or models.

subject: Math
description: Use decimal notation for fractions with denominators 10 or 100.
description_plain: Your child can write fractions with denominators of 10 or 100 as decimals.

subject: Math
description: Express a fraction with denominator 10 as an equivalent fraction with denominator 100, and use this technique to add two fractions with respective denominators 10 and 100.
description_plain: Your child can change a fraction with denominator 10 into an equal fraction with denominator 100, and use this to add fractions with denominators 10 and 100.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving multiplication of a fraction by a whole number.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems where they multiply a fraction by a whole number.

subject: Math
description: Understand a multiple of a/b as a multiple of 1/b, and use this understanding to multiply a fraction by a whole number.
description_plain: Your child understands that multiplying a fraction by a whole number means multiplying the numerator and can use this to find the product.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction a/b as a multiple of 1/b.
description_plain: Your child understands that a fraction like a/b means a times the size of 1/b.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication to multiply a fraction by a whole number.
description_plain: Your child can multiply a fraction by a whole number using what they already know about multiplication.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions referring to the same whole and having like denominators, e.g. by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that add or subtract fractions with the same denominator.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract mixed numbers with like denominators, e.g. by replacing each mixed number with an equivalent fraction (simplest form is not an expectation), and/or by using properties of operations and the relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract mixed numbers with the same bottom number by changing them into fractions or using math rules.

subject: Math
description: Decompose a fraction into a sum of fractions with the same denominator in more than one way, recording each decomposition by an equation. Justify decompositions, e.g. by using a visual fraction model.
description_plain: Your child can break a fraction into a sum of smaller fractions with the same denominator in different ways and explain using pictures.

subject: Math
description: Understand addition and subtraction of fractions as joining and separating parts referring to the same whole.
description_plain: Your child understands adding and subtracting fractions as putting parts together or taking parts apart when they refer to the same whole.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction a/b with a > 1 as a sum of fractions 1/b.
description_plain: Your child understands that a fraction with numerator greater than 1 is the same as adding several fractions with numerator 1.

subject: Math
description: Compare two fractions with different numerators and different denominators, (e.g. by creating common numerators or denominators, or by comparing to a benchmark fraction such as ½.) Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two fractions refer to the same whole. Record the results of comparisons with relational symbols >, <, =, or ≠, and justify the conclusions, (e.g. by using visual fraction models.).
description_plain: Your child can compare two fractions with different top and bottom numbers by making them have the same top or bottom or comparing to ½, and explain which is bigger or if they are equal.

subject: Math
description: Explain why a fraction a/b is equivalent to a fraction ((n·a))/((n·b) ) by using visual fraction models, with attention to how the number and size of the parts differ even though the two fractions themselves are the same size. Use this principle to recognize and generate equivalent fractions.
description_plain: Your child understands why two fractions are equal when both top and bottom numbers are multiplied by the same number, using pictures to show it.

subject: Math
description: Recognize a line of symmetry for a two-dimensional figure as a line across the figure such that the figure can be folded along the line into matching parts. Identify line-symmetric figures and draw lines of symmetry.
description_plain: Your child can find and draw lines of symmetry on flat shapes, showing where the shape can be folded to match perfectly.

subject: Math
description: Classify two-dimensional figures based on the presence or absence of parallel or perpendicular lines, or the presence or absence of angles (right, acute, obtuse, straight, reflex). Recognize and categorize triangles based on angles (acute, obtuse, equiangular, and right) and/or sides (scalene, isosceles, and equilateral).
description_plain: Your child can sort flat shapes by whether they have parallel or perpendicular lines or certain angle sizes, and can recognize and name right triangles.

subject: Math
description: Draw points, lines, line segments, rays, angles (right, acute, obtuse, straight, reflex), and perpendicular and parallel lines. Identify these in two-dimensional figures.
description_plain: Your child can draw and identify points, lines, line segments, rays, different types of angles, and parallel or perpendicular lines in flat shapes.

subject: Math
description: Apply the area and perimeter formulas for rectangles in real world and mathematical problems explaining and justifying the appropriate unit of measure.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas to find the area and perimeter of rectangles and explain which units to use.

subject: Math
description: Use the four operations to solve word problems involving distances, intervals of time, liquid volumes, masses of objects, and money, including problems involving simple fractions or decimals, and problems that require expressing measurements given in a larger unit in terms of a smaller unit. Represent measurement quantities using diagrams such as number line diagrams that feature a measurement scale.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with measurements like distance, time, volume, weight, and money.

subject: Math
description: Know relative sizes of measurement units within one system of units including km, m, cm; kg, g; lb, oz; l, ml; hr, min, sec. Within a single system of measurement, express measurements in a larger unit in terms of a smaller unit. Record measurement equivalents in a two-column table.
description_plain: Your child knows how different units of measurement relate within the same system and can convert measurements from larger to smaller units and make a conversion table.

subject: Math
description: Make a data display (line plot, bar graph, pictograph) to show a set of measurements in fractions of a unit (½, ¼, 1/8, 1/16). Solve problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions by using information presented in the data display.
description_plain: Your child can make graphs to show measurements using fractions and solve addition and subtraction problems using the graph.

subject: Math
description: Find whole-number quotients and remainders with up to four-digit dividends and one-digit divisors, using strategies based on place value, the properties of operations, and/or the relationship between multiplication and division. Illustrate and explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, and/or area models.
description_plain: Your child can divide numbers up to four digits by one-digit numbers, find the quotient and remainder, and explain the steps using pictures or equations.

subject: Math
description: Multiply a whole number of up to four digits by a one-digit whole number, and multiply two two-digit numbers, using strategies based on place value and the properties of operations. Illustrate and explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, and/or area models.
description_plain: Your child can multiply a number up to four digits by a one-digit number and multiply two two-digit numbers, explaining their work with pictures or equations.

subject: Math
description: Fluently (efficiently, accurately, and flexibly) add and subtract multi-digit whole numbers using an efficient algorithm (including, but not limited to: traditional, partial-sums, etc.), based on place value understanding and the properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can quickly and accurately add and subtract large whole numbers using methods based on place value.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding to round multi-digit whole numbers to any place.
description_plain: Your child can round large whole numbers to any place value using their understanding of place value.

subject: Math
description: Read and write multi-digit whole numbers using base-ten numerals, number names, expanded form, and unit form. Compare two multi-digit numbers based on meanings of the digits in each place, using >, <, =, and ≠ symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can read and write large numbers in different ways and compare two numbers using >, <, =, or ≠ by looking at each digit.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that in a multi-digit whole number, a digit in one place represents ten times what it represents in the place to its right.
description_plain: Your child understands that in a number, a digit in one place is ten times bigger than the same digit in the place to its right.

subject: Math
description: Generate a number or shape pattern that follows a given rule. Identify apparent features of the pattern that were not explicit in the rule itself. For example, given the rule "Add 3" and the starting number 1, generate terms in the resulting sequence and observe that the terms appear to alternate between odd and even numbers. Explain informally why the numbers will continue to alternate in this way.
description_plain: Your child can create number or shape patterns that follow a rule and notice new features in the pattern.

subject: Math
description: Find all factor pairs for a whole number in the range 1 to 100. Recognize that a whole number is a multiple of each of its factors. Determine whether a given whole number in the range 1 to 100 is a multiple of a given one-digit number. Determine whether a given whole number in the range 1 to 100 is prime or composite.
description_plain: Your child can find all factor pairs of numbers from 1 to 100, know that numbers are multiples of their factors, and tell if a number is prime or composite.

subject: Math
description: Solve multi-step word problem posed with whole numbers and having whole number answers using the four operations, including problems in which remainders must be interpreted. Represent these problems using situation equations and/or solution equations with a letter or symbol standing for the unknown quantity. Assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies including rounding.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems with several steps using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with whole numbers, including figuring out what to do with remainders, and can check if answers make sense by estimating.

subject: Math
description: Multiply or divide to solve word problems involving multiplicative comparison, (e.g. by using drawings and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem, distinguishing multiplicative comparison from additive comparison.)
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that compare amounts by multiplying or dividing, and can use drawings or equations with a symbol for the unknown number to explain the problem.

subject: Math
description: Interpret a multiplication equation as a comparison, (e.g. interpret 35 = 5·7 as a statement that 35 is 5 times as many as 7 and 7 times as many as 5.) Represent verbal statements of multiplicative comparisons as multiplication equations.
description_plain: Your child understands multiplication equations as comparisons, like knowing 35 = 5 × 7 means 35 is 5 times as many as 7, and can write multiplication equations from words that compare amounts.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the best tools to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics
description_plain: Model with mathematics

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Solve real world problems involving division of unit fractions by non-zero whole numbers and division of whole numbers by unit fractions, e.g. by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve real problems dividing unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions using pictures and equations.

subject: Math
description: Interpret division of a whole number by a unit fraction, and compute such quotients.
description_plain: Your child understands and can calculate dividing a whole number by a unit fraction.

subject: Math
description: Interpret division of a unit fraction by a non-zero whole number, and compute such quotients.
description_plain: Your child understands and can calculate dividing a unit fraction by a whole number.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of division, to divide unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions. Division of a fraction by a fraction is not a requirement at this grade.
description_plain: Your child can divide fractions that are one part (unit fractions) by whole numbers and divide whole numbers by unit fractions.

subject: Math
description: Solve real world problems involving multiplication of fractions and mixed numbers, (e.g. by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem).
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life problems by multiplying fractions and mixed numbers, using pictures or equations to show the problem.

subject: Math
description: Explain why multiplying a given number by a fraction greater than 1 results in a product greater than the given number (recognizing multiplication by whole numbers greater than 1 as a familiar case); explain why multiplying a given number by a fraction less than 1 results in a product smaller than the given number; and relating the principle of fraction equivalence a/b = na/nb to the effect of multiplying a/b by 1.
description_plain: Your child can explain why multiplying a number by a fraction bigger than 1 makes a bigger number, and why multiplying by a fraction less than 1 makes a smaller number, and understands how multiplying a fraction by 1 keeps it the same.

subject: Math
description: Comparing the size of a product to the size of one factor based on the size of the other factor, without performing the indicated multiplication (e.g. They see (½·3) as half the size of 3.).
description_plain: Your child can tell if a multiplication answer will be bigger or smaller than one of the numbers being multiplied, without actually doing the multiplication.

subject: Math
description: Interpret multiplication as scaling (resizing), by:
description_plain: Your child understands multiplication as resizing or scaling numbers.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of a rectangle with fractional side lengths by tiling it with unit squares of the appropriate unit fraction side lengths, and show that the area is the same as would be found by multiplying the side lengths. Multiply fractional side lengths to find areas of rectangles, and represent fraction products as rectangular areas.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of rectangles with fractional sides by counting small squares and by multiplying the side lengths.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the product a/b·q as a parts of a partition of q into b equal parts; equivalently, as the result of a sequence of operations a·q÷b.
description_plain: Your child understands that multiplying a fraction by a number means taking parts of that number, and can think of it as multiplying then dividing.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication to multiply a fraction or whole number by a fraction.
description_plain: Your child can multiply fractions and whole numbers by fractions, building on what they already know about multiplication.

subject: Math
description: Interpret a fraction as division of the numerator by the denominator (a/b = a÷b). Solve word problems involving division of whole numbers leading to answers in the form of fractions or mixed numbers, e.g. by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child understands that a fraction means dividing the top number by the bottom number, and can solve word problems where dividing whole numbers gives answers as fractions or mixed numbers using pictures or equations.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions referring to the same whole, including cases of unlike denominators, (e.g. by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem.) Use benchmark fractions and number sense of fractions to estimate mentally and assess the reasonableness of answers.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems adding and subtracting fractions with different denominators, using pictures or equations to help.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract fractions with unlike denominators (including mixed numbers) by replacing given fractions with equivalent fractions in such a way as to produce an equivalent sum or difference of fractions with like denominators.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract fractions with different denominators by changing them to equivalent fractions with the same denominator.

subject: Math
description: Classify two-dimensional figures in a hierarchy based on properties.
description_plain: Your child can sort flat shapes into groups based on their features, showing how some shapes are part of bigger groups.

subject: Math
description: Understand that attributes belonging to a category of two-dimensional figures also belong to all subcategories of that category.
description_plain: Your child understands that if a shape belongs to a group, it also has all the features of that group.

subject: Math
description: Represent real world and mathematical problems by graphing points in the first quadrant of the coordinate plane, and interpret coordinate values of points in the context of the situation. (e.g. plotting the relationship between two positive quantities such as maps, coordinate grid games (such as Battleship), time/temperature, time/distance, cost/quantity, etc.).
description_plain: Your child can plot points on the first quadrant of a coordinate plane and explain what the points mean in real situations.

subject: Math
description: Use a pair of perpendicular number lines, called axes, to define a coordinate system, with the intersection of the lines (the origin) arranged to coincide with the 0 on each line and a given point in the plane located by using an ordered pair of numbers, called its coordinates. Understand that the first number indicates how far to travel from the origin in the direction of one axis, and the second number indicates how far to travel in the direction of the second axis, with the convention that the names of the two axes and the coordinates correspond (e.g. x-axis and x-coordinate, y-axis and y-coordinate).
description_plain: Your child understands how to use two number lines that cross to find and name points using pairs of numbers called coordinates.

subject: Math
description: Recognize volume as additive. Find volumes of solid figures composed of two non-overlapping right rectangular prisms by adding the volumes of the non-overlapping parts, applying this technique to solve real world problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the volume of solid shapes made by joining two rectangular boxes by adding their volumes, and use this to solve real problems.

subject: Math
description: Apply the formulas V = l·w·h and V = B·h (B represents the area of the base) for rectangular prisms to find volumes of right rectangular prisms with whole-number edge lengths in the context of solving real world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the volume of rectangular boxes by multiplying length, width, and height, or by multiplying the base area by the height, using whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Find the volume of a right rectangular prism with whole-number side lengths by packing it with unit cubes, and show that the volume is the same as would be found by multiplying the edge lengths, equivalently by multiplying the height by the area of the base. Represent three-dimensional whole-number products as volumes, (e.g. to represent the associative property of multiplication.)
description_plain: Your child can find the volume of a rectangular box by filling it with unit cubes and show that multiplying its length, width, and height gives the same volume.

subject: Math
description: Relate volume to the operations of multiplication and addition and solve real world and mathematical problems involving volume.
description_plain: Your child understands that volume can be found by multiplying and adding, and can solve problems about volume.

subject: Math
description: Measure volumes by counting unit cubes such as cubic cm, cubic in, cubic ft. or non-standard cubic units.
description_plain: Your child can measure volume by counting cubes that fill a space, like cubic centimeters or cubic inches.

subject: Math
description: A solid figure which can be packed without gaps or overlaps using n unit cubes is said to have a volume of n cubic units.
description_plain: Your child knows that if a solid can be filled exactly with n unit cubes, its volume is n cubic units.

subject: Math
description: A cube with side length 1 unit, called a "unit cube," is said to have "one cubic unit" of volume, and can be used to measure volume.
description_plain: Your child understands that a cube with sides of 1 unit is called a unit cube and has a volume of one cubic unit.

subject: Math
description: Recognize volume as an attribute of solid figures and understand concepts of volume measurement.
description_plain: Your child understands that volume is how much space a solid figure takes up and learns how to measure it.

subject: Math
description: Make a data display (line plot, bar graph, pictograph) to show a data set of measurements in fractions of a unit (½, ¼, 1/8). Use operations (add, subtract, multiply) on fractions for this grade to solve problems involving information presented in the data display.
description_plain: Your child can make graphs like line plots or bar graphs to show measurements that include fractions, and use adding, subtracting, or multiplying fractions to solve problems with the data.

subject: Math
description: Convert among different-sized standard measurement units within a given measurement system (e.g. convert 5 cm to 0.05 m), and use these conversions in solving multi-step, real world problems.
description_plain: Your child can change measurements from one unit to another within the same system, like centimeters to meters, and use these changes to solve real-world problems with several steps.

subject: Math
description: Add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals to hundredths, using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; relate the strategy to a written method and explain the reasoning used.
description_plain: Your child can add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals to hundredths using drawings and place value strategies, and explain how they solve these problems.

subject: Math
description: Find whole-number quotients of whole numbers with up to four-digit dividends and two-digit divisors, using strategies based on place value, the properties of operations, and/or the relationship between multiplication and division. Illustrate and explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, and/or area models.
description_plain: Your child can divide whole numbers up to four digits by two-digit numbers using place value and multiplication ideas, and explain their work with pictures or equations.

subject: Math
description: Fluently (efficiently, accurately, and flexibly) multiply multi-digit whole numbers using an efficient algorithm (ex., traditional, partial products, etc.) based on place value understanding and the properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can multiply large whole numbers quickly and correctly using methods that show understanding of place value and math rules.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding to round decimals to any place
description_plain: Your child can round decimal numbers to any place value using their understanding of place value.

subject: Math
description: Compare two decimals to thousandths based on meanings of the digits in each place, using >, <, =, and ≠ relational symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can compare two decimal numbers up to thousandths place and use symbols like >, <, =, or ≠ to show which is bigger, smaller, or if they are equal.

subject: Math
description: Read and write decimals to thousandths using base-ten numerals, number names, expanded form, and unit form.
description_plain: Your child can read and write decimal numbers up to thousandths in different ways, like numbers, words, expanded form, and unit form.

subject: Math
description: Read, write, and compare decimals to thousandths.
description_plain: Your child can read, write, and compare decimals up to the thousandths place.

subject: Math
description: Explain patterns in the number of zeros of the product when multiplying a number by powers of 10, and explain patterns in the placement of the decimal point when a decimal is multiplied or divided by a power of 10. Use whole-number exponents to denote powers of 10.
description_plain: Your child can explain patterns when multiplying or dividing numbers by 10, 100, 1000, and use exponents to show these powers of 10.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that in a multi-digit number, a digit in one place represents 10 times as much as it represents in the place to its right and 1/10 of what it represents in the place to its left.
description_plain: Your child knows that in a number, each digit is ten times bigger than the digit to its right and one-tenth the size of the digit to its left.

subject: Math
description: Write simple expressions that record calculations with numbers, and interpret numerical expressions without evaluating them.
description_plain: Your child can write simple math expressions to show calculations and understand what the expressions mean without solving them.

subject: Math
description: Use parentheses in numerical expressions and evaluate expressions with these symbols.
description_plain: Your child can use parentheses in math expressions and solve problems that include these symbols.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the best tools to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics
description_plain: Model with mathematics

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Compare two fractions with the same numerator or the same denominator by reasoning about their size. Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two fractions refer to the same whole. Record the results of comparisons with the relational symbols >, <, =, or ≠, and justify the conclusions, (e.g. by using a visual fraction model.)
description_plain: Your child can compare two fractions that have the same top number or the same bottom number by thinking about their size, knowing the fractions must be parts of the same whole.

subject: Math
description: Express whole numbers as fractions, and recognize fractions that are equivalent to whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can write whole numbers as fractions and recognize when fractions are equal to whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and generate simple equivalent fractions, (e.g. ½ = 2/4, 4/6 = 2/3.) Explain why the fractions are equivalent, e.g. by using a visual fraction model.
description_plain: Your child can find and make simple equivalent fractions, like 1/2 = 2/4, and explain why they are equal using pictures.

subject: Math
description: Understand two fractions as equivalent (equal) if they are the same size, or the same point on a number line.
description_plain: Your child understands that two fractions are equal if they represent the same amount or point on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Explain equivalence of fractions, and compare fractions by reasoning about their size (it is a mathematical convention that when comparing fractions, the whole is the same size).
description_plain: Your child understands when fractions are equal and can compare fractions by thinking about their sizes, knowing the whole they belong to is the same size.

subject: Math
description: Represent a fraction a/b on a number line diagram by marking off a lengths 1/b from 0. Recognize that the resulting interval has size a/b and that its endpoint locates the number a/b on the number line (a is the countable units of 1/b that determines the place on the number line).
description_plain: Your child can show a fraction a/b on a number line by marking off a parts of size 1/b from zero.

subject: Math
description: Represent a fraction 1/b on a number line diagram by defining the interval from 0 to 1 as the whole and partitioning it into b equal parts. Recognize that each part has size 1/b and that the endpoint of the part based at 0 locates the number 1/b on the number line.
description_plain: Your child can show the fraction 1/b on a number line by dividing the space from 0 to 1 into b equal parts.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction as a number on the number line; represent fractions on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child understands fractions as numbers on a number line and can show fractions on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction 1/b as the quantity formed by 1 part when a whole is partitioned into b equal parts; understand a fraction a/b as the quantity formed by a parts of size 1/b.
description_plain: Your child understands that 1/b means one part when a whole is split into b equal parts, and a/b means a parts of size 1/b.

subject: Math
description: Solve real world and mathematical problems involving perimeters of polygons, including finding the perimeter given the side lengths, finding an unknown side length, and exhibiting rectangles with the same perimeter and different areas or with the same area and different perimeters.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about the perimeter of shapes by adding side lengths, finding missing sides, and exploring shapes with the same perimeter but different areas or the same area but different perimeters.

subject: Math
description: Recognize area as additive. Find areas of rectilinear figures by decomposing them into non-overlapping rectangles and adding the areas of the non-overlapping parts, applying this technique to solve real world problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of shapes made of rectangles by breaking them into smaller rectangles, finding each area, and adding them together to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Use tiling to show in a concrete case that the area of a rectangle with whole-number side lengths a and b+c is the sum of a·b and a·c. Use area models to represent the distributive property in mathematical reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can use tiles to show that the area of a rectangle with side lengths a and b+c is the same as adding the areas of rectangles with sides a and b, and a and c, and use this to understand math properties.

subject: Math
description: Multiply side lengths to find areas of rectangles with whole-number side lengths in the context of solving real world and mathematical problems, and represent whole-number products as rectangular areas in mathematical reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of rectangles by multiplying side lengths and use this to solve real-world problems.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of a rectangle with whole-number side lengths by tiling it, and show that the area is the same as would be found by multiplying the side lengths.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of a rectangle by covering it with square tiles and see that this matches multiplying the side lengths.

subject: Math
description: Relate area to the operations of multiplication and addition
description_plain: Your child understands that area is related to multiplication and addition.

subject: Math
description: Measure areas by counting unit squares (square cm, square m, square in, square ft, and non-standard square units).
description_plain: Your child can measure area by counting square units like square centimeters or square inches.

subject: Math
description: A plane figure which can be covered without gaps or overlaps by n unit squares is said to have an area of n square units (does not require standard square units).
description_plain: Your child understands that a flat shape covered exactly by n unit squares has an area of n square units.

subject: Math
description: A square with side length 1 unit, called "a unit square," is said to have "one square unit" of area, and can be used to measure area (does not require standard square units).
description_plain: Your child knows that a square with sides one unit long is called a unit square and has an area of one square unit, which can be used to measure area.

subject: Math
description: Recognize area as an attribute of plane figures and understand concepts of area measurement.
description_plain: Your child understands that area is a property of flat shapes and learns how to measure it.

subject: Math
description: Partition shapes into parts with equal areas. Express the area of each part as a unit fraction of the whole.
description_plain: Your child can divide shapes into equal parts and say the area of each part as a fraction of the whole shape.

subject: Math
description: Understand that shapes in different categories (e.g. rhombuses, rectangles, trapezoids, kites and others) may share attributes (e.g. having four sides), and that the shared attributes can define a larger category (e.g. quadrilaterals). Recognize rhombuses, rectangles, and squares as examples of quadrilaterals, and draw examples of quadrilaterals that do not belong to any of these subcategories.
description_plain: Your child understands that shapes like rhombuses, rectangles, and trapezoids can share features like having four sides, and these shared features put them in bigger groups like quadrilaterals; your child can recognize and draw examples of these shapes.

subject: Math
description: Add, subtract, multiply, or divide to solve one-step word problems involving masses or volumes that are given in the same units, (e.g. by using drawings (such as a beaker with a measurement scale) to represent the problem.)
description_plain: Your child can solve one-step word problems about weight or volume using addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division, and can use drawings like measuring cups to help.

subject: Math
description: Measure and estimate liquid volumes and masses of objects using standard units of grams (g), kilograms (kg), and liters (l) (Excludes cubed units such as cm³ and finding the geometric volume of a container).
description_plain: Your child can measure and estimate liquid volumes and weights using grams, kilograms, milliliters, and liters.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time to the nearest minute using a.m. and p.m. and measure time intervals in minutes. Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of time intervals in minutes, (e.g. by representing the problem on a number line diagram.)
description_plain: Your child can tell and write time to the nearest minute using a.m. and p.m., measure time intervals in minutes, and solve problems adding or subtracting time using number lines.

subject: Math
description: Generate measurement data by measuring lengths using rulers marked with halves and fourths of an inch. Show the data by making a line plot, where the horizontal scale is marked off in appropriate units—whole numbers, halves, or quarters.
description_plain: Your child can measure lengths using rulers marked with halves and quarters of an inch, collect the data, and show it on a line plot with the right units.

subject: Math
description: Draw a scaled picture graph and a scaled bar graph to represent a data set with several categories. Solve one- and two-step "how many more" and "how many less" problems using information presented in scaled bar graphs.
description_plain: Your child can draw picture and bar graphs with a scale to show data and solve problems about how much more or less one category has.

subject: Math
description: Multiply one-digit whole numbers by multiples of 10 in the range 10 to 90 (e.g. 9⋅80, 5⋅60) using strategies based on place value and properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can multiply one-digit numbers by multiples of 10 (like 9 × 80) using place value and math rules.

subject: Math
description: Fluently (efficiently, accurately, & flexibly) add and subtract within 1000 using strategies (e.g. composing/decomposing by like base-10 units, using friendly or benchmark numbers, using related equations, compensation, number line, etc.) and algorithms (including, but not limited to: traditional, partial-sums, etc.) based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 1000 quickly and correctly using different strategies and methods based on place value and math rules.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding to round whole numbers to the nearest 10 or 100.
description_plain: Your child can round whole numbers to the nearest 10 or 100 using place value.

subject: Math
description: Identify arithmetic patterns (including patterns in the addition table or multiplication table), and explain them using properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can find patterns in addition and multiplication tables and explain them using math rules.

subject: Math
description: Solve two-step word problems using any of the four operations. Represent these problems using both situation equations and/or solution equations with a letter or symbol standing for the unknown quantity. Assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies including rounding. This standard is limited to problems posed with whole numbers and having whole-number answers.
description_plain: Your child can solve two-step word problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division with whole numbers, write equations with a letter for the unknown, and check if answers make sense by estimating.

subject: Math
description: Fluently (efficiently, accurately, and flexibly) multiply and divide with single digit multiplications and related divisions using strategies (e.g. relationship between multiplication and division, doubles, double and double again, half and then double, etc.) or properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can quickly and accurately multiply and divide single-digit numbers using strategies like doubles or the relationship between multiplication and division.

subject: Math
description: Understand division as an unknown-factor problem.
description_plain: Your child understands division as finding the missing number in a multiplication problem.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to multiply and divide.
description_plain: Your child can use math rules to help multiply and divide numbers more easily.

subject: Math
description: Determine the unknown whole number in a multiplication or division equation by using related equations.
description_plain: Your child can find the missing number in multiplication or division equations by using related facts.

subject: Math
description: Use multiplication and division within 100 to solve word problems in situations involving equal groups, arrays, and measurement quantities, (e.g. by using drawings and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.)
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems using multiplication and division with numbers up to 100, especially when dealing with equal groups, arrays, or measurements.

subject: Math
description: Interpret whole-number quotients of whole numbers, (e.g. interpret 56÷8 as the number of objects in each share when 56 objects are partitioned equally into 8 shares, or as a number of shares when 56 objects are partitioned into equal shares of 8 objects each.)
description_plain: Your child understands division as splitting a number into equal groups or finding how many groups of a certain size there are.

subject: Math
description: Interpret products of whole numbers, (e.g. interpret 5·7 as the total number of objects in 5 groups of 7 objects each.)
description_plain: Your child can understand multiplication as putting together equal groups, like seeing 5 times 7 as 5 groups of 7 objects.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the best tools to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics
description_plain: Model with mathematics

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Relating the choice of measures of center and variability to the distribution of the data.
description_plain: Your child can choose the best way to describe data by picking the right average and spread based on the data's shape.

subject: Math
description: Giving quantitative measures of center (mean, median and/or mode) and variability (range and/or interquartile range), as well as describing any overall pattern and any striking deviations from the overall pattern with reference to the context in which the data were gathered.
description_plain: Your child can find averages like mean, median, or mode and measures like range to describe data, and explain any unusual patterns using the data's context.

subject: Math
description: Describing the nature of the attribute under investigation, including how it was measured and its units of measurement.
description_plain: Your child can describe what is being measured in data, how it was measured, and the units used.

subject: Math
description: Reporting the number of observations.
description_plain: Your child can tell how many data points or observations there are in a set.

subject: Math
description: Summarize numerical data sets in relation to their context, such as by:
description_plain: Your child can summarize sets of numbers by explaining what they mean in their real-life context.

subject: Math
description: Display numerical data on dot plots, histograms, stem-and-leaf plots, and box plots. (6.SP.4)
description_plain: Your child can show numbers using dot plots, histograms, stem-and-leaf plots, and box plots.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that a measure of center (mean, median and/or mode) for a numerical data set summarizes all of its values with a single number, while a measure of variation (range and/or interquartile range) describes how its values vary with a single number.
description_plain: Your child understands that averages summarize data with one number, and measures like range show how spread out the data is.

subject: Math
description: Analyze a set of data collected to answer a statistical question with a distribution which can be described by its center (mean, median and/or mode), spread (range and/or interquartile range), and overall shape (cluster, peak, gap, symmetry, skew (data) and/or outlier).
description_plain: Your child can look at data to find its center, how spread out it is, and its overall shape, like where it clusters or if there are gaps.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and generate a statistical question as one that anticipates variability in the data related to the question and accounts for it in the answers.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions about data that expect different answers and understand that the data will vary.

subject: Math
description: Analyze the relationship between the dependent and independent variables using graphs and tables, and relate these to the equation.
description_plain: Your child can use graphs and tables to study how one quantity changes when another changes, and connect this to an equation.

subject: Math
description: Write an equation to express one quantity, thought of as the dependent variable, in terms of the other quantity, thought of as the independent variable.
description_plain: Your child can write an equation showing how one quantity depends on another.

subject: Math
description: Identify the independent and dependent variable.
description_plain: Your child can identify which quantity changes on its own (independent) and which depends on it (dependent).

subject: Math
