description: Use variables to represent two quantities in a real-world problem that change in relationship to one another.
description_plain: Your child can use letters to represent two related amounts that change together in a real-life problem.

subject: Math
description: Write an inequality of the form x > c or x < c to represent a constraint or condition in a real-world or mathematical problem. Recognize that inequalities of the form x > c or x < c have infinitely many solutions; represent solutions of such inequalities on number line diagrams.
description_plain: Your child can write inequalities like x > 5 to show limits in problems and can show all solutions on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Solve one-step equations involving non-negative rational numbers using addition, subtraction, multiplication and division.
description_plain: Your child can solve simple equations with positive numbers using addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division.

subject: Math
description: Use variables to represent numbers and write expressions when solving a real-world or mathematical problem; understand that a variable can represent an unknown number, or, depending on the purpose at hand, any number in a specified set.
description_plain: Your child can use letters to stand for numbers and write expressions to solve problems, knowing letters can mean unknown or any number in a set.

subject: Math
description: Understand solving an equation or inequality as a process of answering a question: which values from a specified set, if any, make the equation or inequality true? Use substitution to determine whether a given number in a specified set makes an equation or inequality true.
description_plain: Your child understands solving an equation means finding which numbers make it true and can check this by plugging numbers in.

subject: Math
description: Apply the properties of operations and combine like terms, with the conventions of algebraic notation, to identify and generate equivalent expressions.
description_plain: Your child can use math rules to combine like terms and rewrite expressions that mean the same thing.

subject: Math
description: Evaluate expressions at specific values of their variables. Include expressions that arise from formulas used in real-world problems. Perform arithmetic operations, including those involving whole-number exponents, in the conventional order when there are no parentheses to specify a particular order (Order of Operations).
description_plain: Your child can find the value of math expressions by plugging in numbers, including those from real-life formulas, and follow the correct order of operations.

subject: Math
description: Identify parts of an expression using mathematical terms (sum, term, product, factor, quotient, coefficient); view one or more parts of an expression as a single entity.
description_plain: Your child can name parts of math expressions like sum, product, and factor, and see parts of an expression as one unit.

subject: Math
description: Write expressions that record operations with numbers and with letters standing for numbers.
description_plain: Your child can write math expressions using numbers and letters that stand for numbers.

subject: Math
description: Write, read, and evaluate expressions in which letters stand for numbers.
description_plain: Your child can read, write, and solve math expressions that use letters to represent numbers.

subject: Math
description: Write and evaluate numerical expressions involving whole-number exponents.
description_plain: Your child can write and solve math expressions that include whole-number exponents.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems by graphing points in all four quadrants of the coordinate plane. Include use of coordinates and absolute value to find distances between points with the same first coordinate or the same second coordinate.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems by graphing points in all four parts of the coordinate plane and find distances between points using coordinates and absolute value.

subject: Math
description: Distinguish comparisons of absolute value from statements about order.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between comparing absolute values and comparing the order of numbers.

subject: Math
description: Explain the absolute value of a rational number as its distance from 0 on the number line; interpret absolute value as magnitude for a positive or negative quantity in a real-world situation.
description_plain: Your child can explain absolute value as how far a number is from zero on a number line and understand it as size whether the number is positive or negative.

subject: Math
description: Write, interpret, and explain statements of order for rational numbers in real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child can write and explain how numbers compare in order using real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Interpret statements of inequality as statements about the relative position of two numbers on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can understand inequality statements as showing where two numbers are located on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Understand ordering and absolute value of rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child understands how to order rational numbers and what absolute value means.

subject: Math
description: Find and position integers and other rational numbers on a horizontal or vertical number line diagram; find and position pairs of integers and other rational numbers on a coordinate plane.
description_plain: Your child can find and place integers and rational numbers on number lines and coordinate planes.

subject: Math
description: Recognize signs of numbers in ordered pairs indicate locations in quadrants of the coordinate plane; recognize that when two ordered pairs differ only by signs, the locations of the points are related by reflections across one or both axes.
description_plain: Your child knows that signs in ordered pairs show where points are on a coordinate plane and that changing signs reflects points across axes.

subject: Math
description: Recognize opposite signs of numbers as indicating locations on opposite sides of 0 on the number line; recognize that the opposite of the opposite of a number is the number itself, (e.g. -(-3)=3) and that 0 is its own opposite.
description_plain: Your child knows that opposite signs mean numbers are on opposite sides of zero and that the opposite of the opposite number is the original number.

subject: Math
description: Understand a rational number as a point on the number line and a coordinate pair as a location on a coordinate plane.
description_plain: Your child understands that numbers can show points on a number line and pairs of numbers show locations on a grid.

subject: Math
description: Explaining the meaning of 0 in each situation.
description_plain: Your child can explain what zero means in different situations.

subject: Math
description: Use positive and negative numbers to represent quantities in real-world contexts,
description_plain: Your child can use positive and negative numbers to show real-life amounts, like temperatures or money.

subject: Math
description: Understand positive and negative numbers to describe quantities having opposite directions or values (e.g. temperature above/below zero, elevation above/below sea level, credits/debits, positive/negative electric charge);
description_plain: Your child understands positive and negative numbers describe amounts going in opposite directions or having opposite values.

subject: Math
description: Find the greatest common factor of two whole numbers less than or equal to 100 and the least common multiple of two whole numbers less than or equal to 12. Use the distributive property to express a sum of two whole numbers 1–100 with a common factor as a multiple of a sum of two whole numbers with no common factor.
description_plain: Your child can find the greatest common factor and least common multiple of numbers and use the distributive property to rewrite sums with common factors.

subject: Math
description: Fluently (efficiently, accurately, and flexibly) add, subtract, multiply, and divide multi-digit decimals using an efficient algorithm for each operation.
description_plain: Your child can quickly and accurately add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals with many digits.

subject: Math
description: Fluently (efficiently, accurately, and flexibly) divide multi-digit numbers using an efficient algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can quickly and accurately divide large numbers using a good method.

subject: Math
description: Interpret and compute quotients of fractions, and solve word problems involving division of fractions by fractions, requiring multiple exposures connecting various concrete and abstract models.
description_plain: Your child can divide fractions by fractions and solve word problems about it using pictures and equations.

subject: Math
description: Use ratio reasoning to convert measurement units; manipulate and transform units appropriately when multiplying or dividing quantities.
description_plain: Your child can use ratios to change measurement units and correctly adjust units when multiplying or dividing.

subject: Math
description: Find a percent of a quantity as a rate per 100 (e.g. 30% of a quantity means 30/100 times the quantity); solve problems involving finding the whole, given a part and the percent.
description_plain: Your child can find a percent of a number and solve problems where you know part of a whole and the percent.

subject: Math
description: Make tables of equivalent ratios relating quantities with whole-number measurements, find the missing values in the tables, and plot the pairs of values on the coordinate plane. Use tables to compare ratios. Solve unit rate problems including those involving unit pricing and constant speed.
description_plain: Your child can make tables of equivalent ratios, find missing numbers, plot these on a graph, compare ratios, and solve problems about unit prices and speeds.

subject: Math
description: Use ratio and rate reasoning to solve real-world and mathematical problems, (e.g. by reasoning about tables of equivalent ratios, tape diagrams, double number line diagram, or using calculations.)
description_plain: Your child can use ratios and rates to solve real-life and math problems by using tables, diagrams, or calculations.

subject: Math
description: Use unit rate language ("for each one", "for every one" and "per") and unit rate notation to demonstrate understanding the concept of a unit rate a/b associated with a ratio a:b with b≠0,
description_plain: Your child can use words like 'for each' and symbols to show understanding of unit rates in ratios.

subject: Math
description: Use ratio language to describe a relationship between two quantities. Distinguish between part-to-part and part-to-whole relationships.
description_plain: Your child can use ratio words to describe how two amounts relate and tell the difference between part-to-part and part-to-whole.

subject: Math
description: Represent three-dimensional figures using nets made up of rectangles and triangles, and use the nets to find the surface area of these figures. Apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can draw flat patterns (nets) made of rectangles and triangles to show 3D shapes and use these to find surface area in real-life problems.

subject: Math
description: Draw polygons whose edges meet at right angles (rectilinear figure polygons) in the coordinate plane given coordinates for the vertices; use coordinates to find the length of a side joining points with the same first coordinate or the same second coordinate. Apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can draw shapes with right angles on a coordinate grid using points and find side lengths when points share an x or y value, solving real problems.

subject: Math
description: Find the volume of a right rectangular prism with fractional edge lengths by applying the formulas V=lwh and V=Bh (B is the area of the base and h is the height) to find volumes of right rectangular prisms with fractional edge lengths in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the volume of rectangular boxes with fractional sides using formulas and solve real-world problems.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of all triangles, special quadrilaterals (including parallelograms, kites and trapezoids), and polygons whose edges meet at right angles (rectilinear figure polygons) by composing into rectangles or decomposing into triangles and other shapes; apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of triangles, special four-sided shapes, and right-angled polygons by breaking them into rectangles or triangles, and use this to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the best tools to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics
description_plain: Model with mathematics

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Describe qualitatively the functional relationship between two quantities by analyzing a graph (e.g. where the function is increasing or decreasing, linear or nonlinear). Sketch a graph that exhibits the qualitative features of a function that has been described verbally.
description_plain: Your child can describe how two quantities relate by looking at a graph and can draw a graph based on a verbal description of a function.

subject: Math
description: Construct a function to model a linear relationship between two quantities. Determine the rate of change and initial value of the function from a description of a relationship or from two (x, y) values, including reading these from a table or from a graph. Interpret the rate of change and initial value of a linear function in terms of the situation it models, and in terms of its graph or a table of values.
description_plain: Your child can create a function to show a straight-line relationship, find the rate of change and starting value from descriptions, tables, or graphs, and explain what they mean.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the equation y=mx+b as defining a linear function, whose graph is a straight line; give examples of functions that are not linear.
description_plain: Your child can understand that y = mx + b is a straight-line rule and can give examples of functions that don’t make straight lines.

subject: Math
description: Compare properties of two linear functions represented in a variety of ways (algebraically, graphically, numerically in tables, or by verbal descriptions).
description_plain: Your child can compare two straight-line functions by looking at their equations, graphs, tables, or descriptions.

subject: Math
description: Explain that a function is a rule that assigns to each input exactly one output. The graph of a function is the set of ordered pairs consisting of an input and the corresponding output.
description_plain: Your child understands a function is a rule that gives one output for each input, and its graph shows all input-output pairs.

subject: Math
description: Use the equation of a linear model to solve problems in the context of bivariate measurement data, interpreting the slope and intercept.
description_plain: Your child can use a linear equation to solve problems with two related measurements and understand what the slope and intercept mean.

subject: Math
description: Know that straight lines are widely used to model relationships between two quantitative variables. For scatter plots that suggest a linear association, informally fit a straight line, and informally assess the model fit by judging the closeness of the data points to the line.
description_plain: Your child knows that straight lines can show relationships between two numbers and can draw a line that fits data points on a scatter plot.

subject: Math
description: Construct and interpret scatter plots for bivariate measurement data to investigate patterns of association between two quantities. Describe patterns such as clustering, outliers, positive or negative association, linear association, and nonlinear association.
description_plain: Your child can make and read scatter plots to find patterns like groups, outliers, or whether two things increase or decrease together.

subject: Math
description: Solve linear equations and inequalities with rational number coefficients, including equations/inequalities whose solutions require expanding and/or factoring expressions using the distributive property and collecting like terms.
description_plain: Your child can solve linear equations and inequalities with fractions and decimals, including those needing expanding or factoring.

subject: Math
description: Give examples of linear equations in one variable with one solution (x = a), infinitely many solutions (a = a), or no solutions (a = b). Show which of these possibilities is the case by successively transforming the given equation into simpler forms, until an equivalent equation of the form x = a, a = a, or a = b results (where a and b are different numbers).
description_plain: Your child can give examples of one-variable linear equations that have one solution, many solutions, or no solutions, and show which by simplifying the equation.

subject: Math
description: Fluently (efficiently, accurately, and flexibly) solve one-step, two-step, and multi-step linear equations and inequalities in one variable, including situations with the same variable appearing on both sides of the equal sign.
description_plain: Your child can solve one-step to multi-step linear equations and inequalities with one variable, even when the variable is on both sides.

subject: Math
description: Describe the relationship between the proportional relationship expressed in y = mx and the non-proportional linear relationship y = mx + b as a result of a vertical translation.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the graph y = mx + b is like y = mx moved up or down, showing the difference between proportional and non-proportional relationships.

subject: Math
description: Use similar triangles to explain why the slope (m) is the same between any two distinct points on a non-vertical line in the coordinate plane and extend to include the use of the slope formula (m = y<sub>2</sub> - y<sub>1</sub>/x<sub>2</sub> - x<sub>1</sub>when given two coordinate points (x<sub>1</sub>, y<sub>1</sub>) and (x<sub>2</sub>, y<sub>2</sub>)). Generate the equation y = mx for a line through the origin (proportional) and the equation y = mx + b for a line with slope m intercepting the vertical axis at y-intercept b (not proportional when b ≠ 0).
description_plain: Your child can use triangles to understand why the slope is the same between any two points on a line and write equations for lines that go through the origin or have a vertical intercept.

subject: Math
description: Graph proportional relationships, interpreting its unit rate as the slope (m) of the graph. Compare two different proportional relationships represented in different ways.
description_plain: Your child can graph proportional relationships, understand the slope as the unit rate, and compare different proportional relationships shown in various ways.

subject: Math
description: Read and write numbers expressed in scientific notation, including problems where both decimal and scientific notation are used. Use scientific notation and choose units of appropriate size for measurements of very large or very small quantities (e.g. use millimeters per year for seafloor spreading). Interpret scientific notation that has been generated by technology.
description_plain: Your child can read and write very large or small numbers using scientific notation and choose the right units for measurements, including understanding scientific notation from technology.

subject: Math
description: Use numbers expressed in the form of a single digit times an integer power of 10 to estimate very large or very small quantities, and to express how many times as much one is than the other.
description_plain: Your child can use numbers written as a digit times a power of 10 to estimate very big or small amounts and compare how many times one amount is bigger than another.

subject: Math
description: Use square root and cube root symbols to represent solutions to equations of the form x² = p and x³ = p, where p is a positive rational number. Evaluate square roots of whole number perfect squares with solutions between 0 and 15 and cube roots of whole number perfect cubes with solutions between 0 and 5. Know that √2 is irrational.
description_plain: Your child can use square root and cube root symbols to solve equations, find roots of perfect squares and cubes, and understand that some roots like √2 cannot be written as simple fractions.

subject: Math
description: Use rational approximations of irrational numbers to compare the size of irrational numbers, locate them approximately on a number line diagram, and estimate the value of expressions (e.g. π²).
description_plain: Your child can estimate and compare irrational numbers by using close rational numbers and place them roughly on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Know that numbers that are not rational are called irrational. Understand informally that every number has a decimal expansion; for rational numbers show that the decimal expansion repeats eventually, and convert a decimal expansion which repeats eventually into a rational number.
description_plain: Your child understands that irrational numbers are numbers that can't be written as fractions and can explain how decimals repeat for rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving arc length, area of two-dimensional shapes including sectors, volume and surface area of three-dimensional objects including pyramids, cones and spheres.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about lengths of arcs, areas of shapes including parts of circles, and volumes and surface areas of 3D shapes like pyramids, cones, and spheres.

subject: Math
description: Generalize surface area formula of pyramids and cones (SA = B + ½Pl).
description_plain: Your child can explain and use the formula to find the surface area of pyramids and cones.

subject: Math
description: Generalize the volume formula for pyramids and cones (V = 1/3Bh).
description_plain: Your child can explain and use the formula to find the volume of pyramids and cones.

subject: Math
description: Investigate the relationship between the formulas of three dimensional geometric shapes;
description_plain: Your child can explore how the formulas for different 3D shapes are related.

subject: Math
description: Use the formulas or informal reasoning to find the arc length, areas of sectors, surface areas and volumes of pyramids, cones, and spheres.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas or reasoning to find arc lengths, areas of sectors, surface areas, and volumes of pyramids, cones, and spheres.

subject: Math
description: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points in a coordinate system.
description_plain: Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points on a coordinate grid.

subject: Math
description: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to determine unknown side lengths in right triangles in real-world and mathematical problems in two and three dimensions.
description_plain: Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find missing side lengths in right triangles in real-life and math problems, including in 3D situations.

subject: Math
description: Explain a proof of the Pythagorean Theorem and its converse.
description_plain: Your child can explain the proof of the Pythagorean Theorem and understand its reverse.

subject: Math
description: Draw (freehand, with ruler and protractor, and with technology) geometric shapes with given conditions. Focus on drawing triangles from three measures of angles or sides, noticing when the conditions determine a unique triangle, more than one triangle, or no triangle.
description_plain: Your child can draw geometric shapes by hand, with tools, or technology, especially triangles using given angles or sides, and know when these measurements make one, more than one, or no triangle.

subject: Math
description: Use informal arguments to establish facts about the angle sum and exterior angle of triangles, about the angles created when parallel lines are cut by a transversal, and the angle-angle criterion for similarity of triangles.
description_plain: Your child can explain facts about triangle angles, angles made by parallel lines, and how to tell if triangles are similar.

subject: Math
description: Use facts about supplementary, complementary, vertical, and adjacent angles in a multi-step problem to write and use them to solve simple equations for an unknown angle in a figure.
description_plain: Your child can use facts about different types of angles to solve multi-step problems and find unknown angles in figures.

subject: Math
description: Recognize angle measure as additive. When an angle is decomposed into non-overlapping parts, the angle measure of the whole is the sum of the angle measures of the parts. Solve addition and subtraction problems to find unknown angles on a diagram in real world and mathematical problems, e.g. by using an equation with a symbol for the unknown angle measure.
description_plain: Your child understands that angles can be split into parts and the whole angle is the sum of its parts, and can solve problems to find missing angles using addition and subtraction.

subject: Math
description: Measure angles in whole-number degrees using a protractor. Draw angles of specified measure using a protractor and straight edge.
description_plain: Your child can measure angles in whole degrees using a protractor and draw angles of a specific size using a protractor and straight edge.

subject: Math
description: An angle that turns through n one-degree angles is said to have an angle measure of n degrees.
description_plain: Your child understands that an angle made by turning through n one-degree steps has a measure of n degrees.

subject: Math
description: An angle is measured with reference to a circle with its center at the common endpoint of the rays, by considering the fraction of the circular arc between the points where the two rays intersect the circle. An angle that turns through 1/360 of a circle is called a "one-degree angle," and can be used to measure angles.
description_plain: Your child understands that angles are measured by how much of a circle they cover, with one degree being 1/360 of a full circle.

subject: Math
description: Recognize angles as geometric shapes that are formed wherever two rays share a common endpoint, and understand concepts of angle measurement:
description_plain: Your child understands that angles are shapes made by two rays sharing an endpoint and learns how to measure them.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the best tools to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics
description_plain: Model with mathematics

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Design and use a simulation to generate frequencies for compound events.
description_plain: Your child can create and use simulations to find how often combined events happen.

subject: Math
description: Represent sample spaces for compound events using methods such as organized lists, tables and tree diagrams. For an event described in everyday language (e.g. "rolling double sixes"), identify the outcomes in the sample space which compose the event.
description_plain: Your child can list all possible outcomes for combined events using organized methods and identify which outcomes match a described event.

subject: Math
description: Know that, just as with simple events, the probability of a compound event is the fraction of outcomes in the sample space for which the compound event occurs.
description_plain: Your child knows that the chance of combined events happening is the fraction of all possible outcomes where those events occur.

subject: Math
description: Find probabilities of compound events using organized lists, tables, tree diagrams, and simulation.
description_plain: Your child can find the probability of combined events using lists, tables, tree diagrams, and simulations.

subject: Math
description: Develop a probability model (which may not be uniform) by observing frequencies in data generated from a chance process.
description_plain: Your child can create a probability model by watching how often things happen in chance activities, even if outcomes are not all equally likely.

subject: Math
description: Develop a uniform probability model by assigning equal probability to all outcomes, and use the model to determine probabilities of events.
description_plain: Your child can make a probability model where all outcomes are equally likely and use it to find the chance of events.

subject: Math
description: Develop a probability model and use it to find probabilities of events. Compare probabilities from a model to observed frequencies; if the agreement is not good, explain possible sources of the discrepancy.
description_plain: Your child can build a probability model to find chances of events and compare these to actual results, explaining why they might be different.

subject: Math
description: Collect data from a chance process (probability experiment). Approximate the probability by observing its long-run relative frequency. Recognize that as the number of trials increase, the experimental probability approaches the theoretical probability. Conversely, predict the approximate relative frequency given the probability.
description_plain: Your child can collect data from chance experiments, estimate probabilities by repeating trials, and understand that more trials make estimates closer to the true probability.

subject: Math
description: Express the probability of a chance event as a number between 0 and 1 that represents the likelihood of the event occurring. (Larger numbers indicate greater likelihood. A probability near 0 indicates an unlikely event, a probability around ½ indicates an event that is neither unlikely nor likely, and a probability near 1 indicates a likely event.)
description_plain: Your child can express how likely an event is by a number between 0 and 1, where 0 means impossible, 1 means certain, and numbers in between show different chances.

subject: Math
description: Use measures of center (mean, median and/or mode) and measures of variability (range, interquartile range and/or mean absolute deviation) for numerical data from random samples to draw informal comparative inferences about two populations.
description_plain: Your child can use averages and measures of spread from samples to compare two groups and make informal conclusions.

subject: Math
description: Informally assess the degree of visual overlap of two numerical data distributions with similar variabilities, measuring the difference between the centers by expressing it as a multiple of a measure of variability (requires introduction of mean absolute deviation).
description_plain: Your child can look at two sets of data and estimate how much their values overlap and how different their centers are compared to their spread.

subject: Math
description: Use data from a random sample to draw inferences about a population with an unknown characteristic of interest. Generate multiple samples (or simulated samples) of the same size to informally gauge the variation in estimates or predictions.
description_plain: Your child can use data from random samples to make guesses about a larger group and create several samples to see how much the guesses might change.

subject: Math
description: Identify if a particular random sample would be representative of a population and justify your reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can decide if a sample fairly represents a whole group and explain why.

subject: Math
description: Know that generalizations about a population from a sample are valid only if the sample is representative of that population and generate a valid representative sample of a population.
description_plain: Your child knows that conclusions about a whole group are only good if the sample fairly represents it and can create a fair sample.

subject: Math
description: Use statistics to gain information about a population by examining a sample of the population;
description_plain: Your child can use data from a sample to learn about a larger group.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems leading to inequalities of the form px+q > r or px+q < r where p, q, and r are specific rational numbers and p > 0. Graph the solution set of the inequality and interpret it in the context of the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that lead to inequalities, graph the solutions, and explain what the solutions mean in the problem.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems leading to equations of the form px+q=r, and p(x+q)=r where p, q, and r are specific rational numbers. Solve equations of these forms fluently (efficiently, accurately, and flexibly). Compare an algebraic solution to an arithmetic solution, identifying the sequence of the operations used in each approach.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that lead to equations, solve them quickly and accurately, and compare algebraic and arithmetic methods.

subject: Math
description: Use variables to represent quantities in a real-world or mathematical problem, and construct two-step equations and inequalities to solve problems by reasoning about the quantities.
description_plain: Your child can use variables to write and solve two-step equations and inequalities from real-world problems.

subject: Math
description: Solve multi-step real-life and mathematical problems with rational numbers. Apply properties of operations to calculate with numbers in any form; convert between forms as appropriate; and assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies.
description_plain: Your child can solve multi-step problems with rational numbers, use properties of operations, change number forms when needed, and check if answers make sense.

subject: Math
description: Understand that rewriting an expression in different forms in a problem context can shed light on the problem and how the quantities in it are related.
description_plain: Your child understands that changing how an expression looks can help understand the problem and how the numbers relate.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add, subtract, factor, and expand linear expressions with integer coefficients.
description_plain: Your child can use math rules to add, subtract, factor, and expand expressions with rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Solve and interpret real-world and mathematical problems involving the four operations with rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can solve and understand problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Convert a rational number in the form of a fraction to its decimal equivalent using long division; know that the decimal form of a rational number terminates in 0s or eventually repeats.
description_plain: Your child can change fractions into decimals using long division and knows that decimals for fractions either end or repeat.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to multiply and divide rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can use math rules to multiply and divide rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Explain that integers can be divided, provided that the divisor is not zero, and every quotient of integers (with non-zero divisor) is a rational number. Leading to situations such that if p and q are integers, then -(p/q)=(-p)/q=p/(-q).
description_plain: Your child understands that dividing integers (except by zero) always gives a rational number and knows rules for negative signs in division.

subject: Math
description: Describe how multiplication is extended from positive rational numbers to all rational numbers by requiring that operations continue to satisfy the properties of operations, particularly the distributive property, leading to products such as (-1)(-1)=1 and the rules for multiplying signed numbers.
description_plain: Your child understands how multiplication works with all rational numbers, including negatives, and knows rules like (-1)(-1) = 1.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division of positive rational numbers to multiply and divide all rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can multiply and divide all rational numbers, building on what they know about positive numbers.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add and subtract rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can use properties of operations to add and subtract rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Model subtraction as the distance between two rational numbers on the number line where the distance is the absolute value of their difference.
description_plain: Your child can think of subtraction as the distance between two numbers on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Model subtraction of rational numbers as adding the additive inverse, p-q=p+(-q).
description_plain: Your child can understand subtraction of rational numbers as adding the opposite number.

subject: Math
description: Show p+q as the number located a distance |q| from p, in the positive or negative direction depending on whether q is positive or negative.
description_plain: Your child can show adding a number means moving that distance on the number line in the right direction depending on if the number is positive or negative.

subject: Math
description: Describe situations in which opposite quantities combine to make 0. Show that a number and its opposite have a sum of 0 (are additive inverses).
description_plain: Your child can explain situations where opposite numbers add up to zero and show that a number plus its opposite equals zero.

subject: Math
description: Represent addition and subtraction on a horizontal or vertical number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can show addition and subtraction by moving along a number line, either left to right or up and down.

subject: Math
description: Use proportional relationships to solve multistep ratio and percent problems. Examples: simple interest, tax, markups and markdowns, gratuities and commissions, fees, percent increase and decrease, percent error.
description_plain: Your child can solve multi-step problems involving ratios and percentages, like calculating interest, tax, tips, discounts, and percent changes.

subject: Math
description: Explain what a point (x, y) on the graph of a proportional relationship means in terms of the situation, with special attention to the points (0, 0) and (1, r) where r is the unit rate.
description_plain: Your child can explain what points on a graph mean in a proportional relationship, especially the points (0,0) and (1, r) where r is the unit rate.

subject: Math
description: Represent proportional relationships by equations. For example, if total cost t is proportional to the number n of items purchased at a constant price p, the relationship between the total cost and the number of items can be expressed as t=pn.
description_plain: Your child can write equations to show proportional relationships, like how total cost equals price times number of items.

subject: Math
description: Analyze a table or graph and recognize that, in a proportional relationship, every pair of numbers has the same unit rate (referred to as the "m").
description_plain: Your child can look at tables or graphs and find the constant rate that shows two quantities are proportional.

subject: Math
description: Determine whether two quantities are in a proportional relationship, e.g. by testing for equivalent ratios in a table or graphing on a coordinate plane and observing whether the graph is a straight line through the origin.
description_plain: Your child can decide if two amounts are proportional by checking if their ratios are equal or if their graph is a straight line through zero.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and represent proportional relationships between quantities:
description_plain: Your child understands how to recognize and show proportional relationships between different amounts.

subject: Math
description: Compute unit rates associated with ratios of fractions, including ratios of lengths, areas and other quantities measured in like or different units.
description_plain: Your child can find unit rates for ratios involving fractions, like speed or area, and write them as complex fractions or simpler numbers.

subject: Math
description: Describe the two-dimensional figures that result from slicing three-dimensional figures, as in plane sections of right rectangular prisms and right cylinder.
description_plain: Your child can describe the flat shapes made when slicing 3D shapes like rectangular boxes and pyramids.

subject: Math
description: Identify three-dimensional objects generated by rotating a two-dimensional (rectangular or triangular) object around one edge.
description_plain: Your child can identify 3D shapes made by spinning 2D shapes like rectangles or triangles around one edge.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving scale drawings of geometric figures, such as computing actual lengths and areas from a scale drawing and reproducing a scale drawing at a different scale.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems using scale drawings, like finding real lengths and areas or making a drawing bigger or smaller.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving area of two-dimensional objects and volume and surface area of three-dimensional objects including cylinders and right prisms. (Solutions should not require students to take square roots or cube roots.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems about the area of flat shapes and the volume and surface area of 3D shapes like cylinders and prisms.

subject: Math
description: Generalize the surface area formula for prisms and cylinders (SA = 2B + Ph where B is the area of the base, P is the perimeter of the base, and h is the height (in the case of a cylinder, perimeter is replaced by circumference)).
description_plain: Your child can explain the formula for surface area of prisms and cylinders using the base area, perimeter, and height.

subject: Math
description: Generalize the volume formula for prisms and cylinders (V = Bh where B is the base and h is the height).
description_plain: Your child can explain the formula for volume of prisms and cylinders using the base area and height.

subject: Math
description: Investigate the relationship between three-dimensional geometric shapes;
description_plain: Your child explores how different 3D shapes are related to each other.

subject: Math
description: .Use the formulas for the area and circumference of a circle and solve problems; give an informal derivation of the relationship between the circumference and area of a circle.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas for the area and circumference of circles to solve problems and understand how these two measurements are connected.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the best tools to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics
description_plain: Model with mathematics

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Analyze decisions and strategies using probability concepts (e.g. product testing, medical testing, pulling a hockey goalie at the end of a game).
description_plain: Your child can use probability to analyze decisions and strategies, like testing products or making game choices.

subject: Math
description: Use probabilities to make fair decisions (e.g. drawing by lots, using a random number generator).
description_plain: Your child can use probability to make fair choices, such as drawing lots or using random number generators.

subject: Math
description: Evaluate and compare strategies on the basis of expected values.
description_plain: Your child can compare different strategies by calculating their expected results.

subject: Math
description: Find the expected payoff for a game of chance.
description_plain: Your child can find the average expected winnings or losses in a game of chance.

subject: Math
description: Weigh the possible outcomes of a decision by assigning probabilities to payoff values and finding expected values.
description_plain: Your child can weigh different outcomes by assigning probabilities and calculating expected values to help make decisions.

subject: Math
description: Develop a probability distribution for a random variable defined for a sample space in which probabilities are assigned empirically; find the expected value.
description_plain: Your child can create a probability distribution based on observed data and find the expected value.

subject: Math
description: Develop a probability distribution for a random variable defined for a sample space in which theoretical probabilities can be calculated; find the expected value.
description_plain: Your child can create a probability distribution using calculated probabilities and find the expected value.

subject: Math
description: Calculate the expected value of a random variable; interpret it as the mean of the probability distribution.
description_plain: Your child can calculate the expected value of a random event and understand it as the average outcome.

subject: Math
description: Define a random variable for a quantity of interest by assigning a numerical value to each event in a sample space; graph the corresponding probability distribution using the same graphical displays as for data distributions.
description_plain: Your child can define a random variable by assigning numbers to outcomes and graph the probability distribution like other data.

subject: Math
description: Use permutations and combinations to compute probabilities of compound events and solve problems.
description_plain: Your child can use counting methods to find probabilities of combined events and solve related problems.

subject: Math
description: Apply the general Multiplication Rule in a uniform probability model, P(A and B) = P(A)P(B│A) = P(B)P(A│B), and interpret the answer in terms of the model.
description_plain: Your child can use the Multiplication Rule to find the probability of two events happening together and explain the result.

subject: Math
description: Apply the Addition Rule, P(A or B) = P(A)+P(B)-P(A and B), and interpret the answer in terms of the model.
description_plain: Your child can use the Addition Rule to find the probability of one event or another happening and explain the result.

subject: Math
description: Find the conditional probability of A given B as the fraction of B's outcomes that also belong to A, and interpret the answer in terms of the model.
description_plain: Your child can find the chance of event A happening given that event B has happened and explain what it means.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and explain the concepts of conditional probability and independence in everyday language and everyday situations.
description_plain: Your child can explain what conditional probability and independence mean using everyday examples.

subject: Math
description: Construct and interpret two-way frequency tables of data when two categories are associated with each object being classified. Use the two-way table as a sample space to decide if events are independent and to approximate conditional probabilities.
description_plain: Your child can make and use two-way tables to see how two categories relate and to estimate probabilities.

subject: Math
description: Understand the conditional probability of A given B as P(A and B)/P(B) , and interpret independence of A and B as saying that the conditional probability of A given B is the same as the probability of A, and the conditional probability of B given A is the same as the probability of B.
description_plain: Your child understands conditional probability and can explain when two events are independent.

subject: Math
description: Understand that two events A and B are independent if the probability of A and B occurring together is the product of their probabilities, and use this characterization to determine if they are independent.
description_plain: Your child knows that two events are independent if the chance of both happening is the product of their chances and can use this to check independence.

subject: Math
description: Describe events as subsets of a sample space (the set of outcomes) using characteristics (or categories) of the outcomes, or as unions, intersections, or complements of other events ("or," "and," "not").
description_plain: Your child can describe events using sets of outcomes and combine events using 'or,' 'and,' and 'not' ideas.

subject: Math
description: Evaluate reports based on data.
description_plain: Your child can judge how reliable reports based on data are.

subject: Math
description: Use data from a randomized experiment to compare two treatments; use simulations to decide if differences between parameters are significant.
description_plain: Your child can use data from experiments to compare treatments and use simulations to see if differences are meaningful.

subject: Math
description: Use data from a sample survey to estimate a population mean or proportion; develop a margin of error, (e.g. through the use of simulation models for random sampling.)
description_plain: Your child can use survey data to estimate averages or proportions and find how much error might be in the estimate.

subject: Math
description: Recognize the purposes of and differences among sample surveys, experiments, and observational studies; explain how randomization relates to each.
description_plain: Your child knows the differences between surveys, experiments, and observational studies and how random choices affect them.

subject: Math
description: Decide if a specified model is consistent with results from a given data-generating process, e.g. using simulation.
description_plain: Your child can decide if a model fits real data by using simulations.

subject: Math
description: Understand statistics as a process for making inferences to be made about population parameters based on a random sample from that population.
description_plain: Your child understands that statistics help make guesses about a whole group based on a random sample.

subject: Math
description: Fit quadratic and exponential functions to the data. Use functions fitted to data to solve problems in the context of the data.
description_plain: Your child can fit quadratic and exponential functions to data and use them to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Assess the fit of a function by plotting and analyzing residuals.
description_plain: Your child can check how well a function fits data by looking at the differences between the data and the function.

subject: Math
description: Use the mean and standard deviation of a data set to fit it to a normal distribution and to estimate population percentages. Recognize that there are data sets for which such a procedure is not appropriate. Use calculators, spreadsheets, and tables to estimate areas under the normal curve.
description_plain: Your child can use the average and spread of data to fit it to a normal curve and estimate percentages, knowing when this method works best.

subject: Math
description: Derive the equations of ellipses and hyperbolas given the foci, using the fact that the sum or difference of distances from the foci is constant; graph the ellipse or hyperbola in the coordinate plane.
description_plain: Your child can find and graph equations for ellipses and hyperbolas using their special distance properties.

subject: Math
description: Derive the equation of a parabola given a focus and directrix; graph the parabola in the coordinate plane.
description_plain: Your child can find and graph the equation of a parabola using its focus and directrix.

subject: Math
description: Complete the square to find the center and radius of a circle given by an equation.
description_plain: Your child can rewrite a circle’s equation to find its center and radius.

subject: Math
description: Derive the equation of a circle of given center and radius using the Pythagorean Theorem; graph the circle in the coordinate plane;
description_plain: Your child can write and graph the equation of a circle using its center and radius.

subject: Math
description: Derive using similarity the fact that the length of the arc intercepted by an angle is proportional to the radius, and define the radian measure of the angle as the constant of proportionality; derive the formula for the area of a sector.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the length of an arc relates to the radius and angle, define radians, and find the area of a sector.

subject: Math
description: Construct inscribed and circumscribed circles for polygons and tangent lines from a point outside a given circle to the circle.
description_plain: Your child can draw circles inside and outside polygons and draw tangent lines from a point outside a circle.

subject: Math
description: Construct inscribed and circumscribed circles for triangles.
description_plain: Your child can draw circles inside and outside triangles.

subject: Math
description: Understand and apply the Law of Sines and the Law of Cosines to find unknown measurements in right and non-right triangles (e.g. surveying problems, resultant forces).
description_plain: Your child can use the Law of Sines and Law of Cosines to find missing parts of any triangle.

subject: Math
description: Prove the Laws of Sines and Cosines and use them to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child can prove the Law of Sines and Law of Cosines and use them to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Derive the formula A = ½ ab sin C for the area of a triangle by drawing an auxiliary line from a vertex perpendicular to the opposite side.
description_plain: Your child can find the formula for the area of a triangle using a line drawn inside the triangle.

subject: Math
description: Use congruence and similarity criteria for triangles to solve problems and to prove relationships in geometric figures.
description_plain: Your child can use rules about when triangles are the same shape or size to solve problems and explain how parts of shapes relate.

subject: Math
description: Construct arguments about triangles using theorems. Theorems include: a line parallel to one side of a triangle divides the other two proportionally, and conversely; the Pythagorean Theorem proved using triangle similarity, and AA.
description_plain: Your child can explain why certain lines and triangles have special properties using important triangle rules like the Pythagorean Theorem.

subject: Math
description: Construct an equilateral triangle, a square, and a regular hexagon inscribed in a circle.
description_plain: Your child can draw an equilateral triangle, a square, and a regular hexagon inside a circle.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate triangle congruence using rigid motion (ASA, SAS, and SSS).
description_plain: Your child can show that two triangles are exactly the same by moving them without changing their shape or size.

subject: Math
description: Given two figures, use the definition of congruence in terms of rigid motions to decide if they are congruent.
description_plain: Your child can decide if two shapes are exactly the same by using movements like sliding, turning, or flipping.

subject: Math
description: Prove the addition and subtraction formulas for sine, cosine, and tangent and use them to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child can prove and use formulas for adding and subtracting sine, cosine, and tangent to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Prove the Pythagorean identity sin²(θ) + cos²(θ) = 1 and use it to find sin(θ), cos(θ), or tan(θ) given sin (θ), cos(θ), or tan(θ) and the quadrant.
description_plain: Your child can prove the Pythagorean identity for sine and cosine and use it to find missing trigonometric values based on the angle's location.

subject: Math
description: Use inverse functions to solve trigonometric equations that arise in modeling contexts; evaluate the solutions using technology, and interpret them in terms of the context.
description_plain: Your child can solve trigonometry problems by using inverse functions and technology, and explain what the answers mean.

subject: Math
description: Understand that restricting a trigonometric function to a domain on which it is always increasing or always decreasing allows its inverse to be constructed.
description_plain: Your child understands that limiting a trig function to a certain part lets you find its inverse function.

subject: Math
description: Choose trigonometric functions to model periodic phenomena with specified amplitude, frequency, and midline.
description_plain: Your child can choose trig functions to model repeating patterns with specific height, speed, and middle value.

subject: Math
description: Use the unit circle to explain symmetry (odd and even) and periodicity of trigonometric functions.
description_plain: Your child can use the unit circle to explain patterns and symmetry in trig functions.

subject: Math
description: Use special triangles to determine geometrically the values of sine, cosine, tangent for π/3, π/4, and π/6, use the unit circle to express the values of sine, cosine, and tangent for π - x, π + x, and 2π - x in terms of their values for x, where x is any real number.
description_plain: Your child can find sine, cosine, and tangent values for special angles using triangles and the unit circle.

subject: Math
description: Explain how the unit circle in the coordinate plane enables the extension of trigonometric functions to all real numbers, interpreted as radian measures of angles traversed counterclockwise around the unit circle.
description_plain: Your child understands how the unit circle helps extend trig functions to all angles measured in radians.

subject: Math
description: Understand radian measure of an angle as the length of the arc on the unit circle subtended by the angle.
description_plain: Your child understands that the radian measure of an angle is the length of the arc it cuts out on a circle with a radius of one.

subject: Math
description: Produce an invertible function from a non-invertible function by restricting the domain.
description_plain: Your child can make a function reversible by limiting the inputs it uses.

subject: Math
description: Verify by composition that one function is the inverse of another.
description_plain: Your child can check that two functions undo each other by combining them.

subject: Math
description: Find inverse functions.
description_plain: Find inverse functions.

subject: Math
description: Write arithmetic and geometric sequences and series both recursively and with an explicit formula, use them to model situations, and translate between the two forms.
description_plain: Your child can write number patterns using formulas and use them to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Use functions to model real-world relationships.
description_plain: Your child can use functions to describe and solve real-life problems.

subject: Math
description: Compare properties of two functions using a variety of representations (algebraically, graphically, numerically in tables, or by verbal descriptions).
description_plain: Your child can compare two functions by looking at their equations, graphs, tables, or descriptions.

subject: Math
description: Write a function in different but equivalent forms to reveal and explain different properties of the function.
description_plain: Your child can write the same function in different ways to show different features.

subject: Math
description: Graph trigonometric functions, showing period, midline, and amplitude.
description_plain: Your child can draw graphs of trig functions showing their repeating patterns and heights.

subject: Math
description: Graph rational functions, identifying zeros and asymptotes when suitable factorizations are available, and showing end behavior.
description_plain: Your child can graph rational functions, showing where they cross zero, their behavior near certain lines, and how they behave far away.

subject: Math
description: Graph piecewise-defined functions, including step functions.
description_plain: Your child can graph functions that are made of different pieces, including step-like graphs.

subject: Math
description: Graph functions expressed symbolically and show key features of the graph, by hand in simple cases and using technology for more complicated cases.
description_plain: Your child can graph functions shown by formulas and identify important features by hand for simple cases and using technology for harder ones.

subject: Math
description: Relate the domain of a function to its graph and, where applicable, to the quantitative relationship it describes.
description_plain: Your child understands how the possible input values of a function relate to its graph and the real-world situation it describes.

subject: Math
description: For a function that models a relationship between two quantities, interpret key features of expressions, graphs and tables in terms of the quantities, and sketch graphs showing key features given a description of the relationship. Key features include: intercepts; intervals where the function is increasing, decreasing, positive, or negative; relative maximums and minimums; symmetries; end behavior; and periodicity.
description_plain: Your child can understand and explain important parts of a function’s graph and equation, like where it goes up or down, crosses axes, or repeats.

subject: Math
description: Use function notation, evaluate functions for inputs in their domains, and interpret statements that use function notation in terms of a context.
description_plain: Your child can use function notation to find outputs for inputs and explain what function statements mean in real situations.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a function from one set (called the domain) to another set (called the range) assigns to each element of the domain exactly one element of the range. If f is a function and x is an element of its domain, then f(x) denotes the output of f corresponding to the input x. The graph of f is the graph of the equation y = f(x).
description_plain: Your child understands that a function matches each input with exactly one output and can read and graph functions using this idea.

subject: Math
description: Understand that the graph of an equation in two variables is the set of all its solutions plotted in the coordinate plane, often forming a curve (which could be a line).
description_plain: Your child understands that the graph of an equation shows all the solutions as points on a plane, often forming a curve or line.

subject: Math
description: Represent a system of linear equations as a single matrix equation and solve (incorporating technology) for matrices of dimension 3 × 3 or greater.
description_plain: Your child can write and solve systems of linear equations using matrices and technology.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understanding to solve equations, inequalities, and compound inequalities in one variable, including literal equations and inequalities. (A.REI.3)
description_plain: Your child can solve different kinds of equations and inequalities, including those with letters instead of numbers.

subject: Math
description: Explain each step in solving a simple equation as following from the equality of numbers asserted at the previous step, starting from the assumption that the original equation has a solution. Construct a viable argument to justify a solution method.
description_plain: Your child can explain each step when solving an equation and give reasons why the steps work.

subject: Math
description: Rearrange formulas to highlight a quantity of interest, using the same reasoning as in solving equations.
description_plain: Your child can rearrange formulas to solve for a specific variable, using the same ideas as solving equations.

subject: Math
description: Represent constraints by equations or inequalities, and by systems of equations and/or inequalities, and interpret solutions as viable or non-viable options in a modeling context.
description_plain: Your child can write equations or inequalities to show limits or rules in a problem and understand which solutions make sense.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understanding to create equations in two or more variables to represent relationships between quantities; graph equations on coordinate axes with labels and scales.
description_plain: Your child can create equations with two or more variables to show relationships and graph them with labels and scales.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understanding to create equations and inequalities in one variable and use them to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child can write and solve equations and inequalities with one variable to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational expressions.
description_plain: Your child can add, subtract, multiply, and divide expressions that have fractions with variables.

subject: Math
description: Rewrite simple rational expressions in different forms; write a(x)/b(x) in the form q(x) + r(x)/b(x), where a(x), b(x), q(x), and r(x) are polynomials with the degree of r(x) less than the degree of b(x), using inspection, long division, or, for the more complicated examples, a computer algebra system.
description_plain: Your child can rewrite rational expressions in simpler forms using division or technology.

subject: Math
description: Know and apply the Binomial Theorem for the expansion of (x + y)² in powers of x and y for a positive integer n, where x and y are any numbers, with coefficients determined for example by Pascal's Triangle. The Binomial Theorem can be proven by mathematical induction or by a combinatorial argument.
description_plain: Your child knows how to expand expressions like (x + y)ⁿ using the Binomial Theorem and patterns like Pascal’s Triangle.

subject: Math
description: Use the structure of an expression to identify ways to rewrite it.
description_plain: Your child can look at how an expression is built to find ways to rewrite it.

subject: Math
description: Interpret complicated expressions by viewing one or more of their parts as a single entity.
description_plain: Your child can understand complicated expressions by treating parts of them as single pieces.

subject: Math
description: Interpret parts of an expression, such as terms, factors, and coefficients.
description_plain: Your child can explain parts of an expression like terms, factors, and numbers multiplying them.

subject: Math
description: Interpret expressions that represent a quantity in terms of its context.
description_plain: Your child can explain what expressions mean in real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: Multiply a vector (regarded as a matrix with one column) by a matrix of suitable dimensions to produce another vector. Work with matrices as transformations of vectors.
description_plain: Your child can multiply a vector by a matrix to get another vector and understand matrices as ways to change vectors.

subject: Math
description: Understand that the zero and identity matrices play a role in matrix addition and multiplication similar to the role of 0 and 1 in the real numbers. The determinant of a square matrix is nonzero if and only if the matrix has a multiplicative inverse.
description_plain: Your child understands that zero and identity matrices act like 0 and 1 in addition and multiplication, and when a matrix can be reversed.

subject: Math
description: Understand that, unlike multiplication of numbers, matrix multiplication for square matrices is not a commutative operation, but still satisfies the associative and distributive properties.
description_plain: Your child knows that multiplying matrices doesn’t always work the same way as numbers but still follows some rules.

subject: Math
description: Compute the magnitude of a scalar multiple cv using ||cv|| = |c|v. Compute the direction of cv knowing that when |c|v ≠ 0, the direction of cv is either along v (for c > 0) or against v (for c < 0).
description_plain: Your child can find the size and direction of a vector after multiplying it by a number, knowing if it points the same way or opposite.

subject: Math
description: Represent scalar multiplication graphically by scaling vectors and possibly reversing their direction; perform scalar multiplication component-wise, (e.g. as c(v<sub>x</sub>, v<sub>y</sub>) = (cv<sub>x</sub>, cv<sub>y</sub>).)
description_plain: Your child can show multiplying a vector by a number by stretching or flipping it and can multiply each part separately.

subject: Math
description: Multiply a vector by a scalar.
description_plain: Your child can multiply a vector by a number.

subject: Math
description: Understand vector subtraction v - w as v + (-w), where –w is the additive inverse of w, with the same magnitude as w and pointing in the opposite direction. Represent vector subtraction graphically by connecting the tips in the appropriate order, and perform vector subtraction component-wise.
description_plain: Your child understands subtracting one vector from another by adding the opposite vector and can show this on a graph and by components.

subject: Math
description: Given two vectors in magnitude and direction form, determine the magnitude and direction of their sum.
description_plain: Your child can find the size and direction when adding two vectors given their sizes and directions.

subject: Math
description: Add vectors end-to-end, component-wise, and by the parallelogram rule . Understand that the magnitude of a sum of two vectors is typically not the sum of the magnitudes.
description_plain: Your child can add vectors in different ways and knows that the length of the sum isn’t just the sum of lengths.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract vectors.
description_plain: Add and subtract vectors.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving velocity and other quantities that can be represented by vectors.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems involving velocity and other quantities that have both size and direction.

subject: Math
description: Find the components of a vector by subtracting the coordinates of an initial point from the coordinates of a terminal point.
description_plain: Your child can find the parts of a vector by subtracting the starting point coordinates from the ending point coordinates.

subject: Math
description: Recognize vector quantities as having both magnitude and direction. Represent vector quantities by directed line segments, and use appropriate symbols for vectors and their magnitudes (e.g., v, |v|, ||v||, v).
description_plain: Your child understands that vectors have size and direction and can draw and label them correctly.

subject: Math
description: Know the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra; show that it is true for quadratic polynomials.
description_plain: Your child knows the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra and can show it works for quadratic equations.

subject: Math
description: Extend polynomial identities to the complex numbers.
description_plain: Your child can extend rules about polynomials to include complex numbers.

subject: Math
description: Calculate the distance between numbers in the complex plane as the modulus of the difference, and the midpoint of a segment as the average of the numbers at its endpoints.
